coredata.dc:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots incorporated cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride ternary composite for electrochemical overall water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Reghunath, B Shalini
Rajasekaran, Sruthi
Devi K R, Sunaja
Pinheiro, Dephan
Jaleel UC, Jadan Resnik
coredata.dc:description: Multicomponent electrocatalysts containing carbon supports play a crucial role in influencing the hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions which enhance the total water splitting. Herein, we report a ternary composite with cobalt ferrite, graphitic carbon nitride, and N-doped graphene quantum dots prepared via hydrothermal technique. The purity of the samples is established by carrying out various characterization methods. The intrinsic characteristics of the obtained materials are investigated by employing electrocatalytic processes in an alkaline media toward hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions. Cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N doped graphene quantum dots electrocatalyst demonstrates a very low overpotential towards hydrogen evolution reaction of 287 mV at a constant 10 mA cm−2 current density in 1.0 M KOH. Tafel slope and Rct values generated are 94 mV dec−1 and 0.86 cm2, respectively. Oxygen evolution reaction studies reveal an overpotential of 445 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with a Tafel slope of 69 mV dec−1. Finally, the cell potential needed for the cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N doped graphene quantum dots electrode to achieve 10 mA cm−2 in total water splitting is only 2.0 V while displaying long-term stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HER and OER
Water splitting
Ternary electrocatalyst
Cobalt ferrite
gCN
N doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of cobalt ferrite/gCN/N doped graphene quantum dots (CGD)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Kanagawa Academy of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: BSR thank Karnataka Science and Technology Academy (KSTA), for the Short-Term Studies grant (KSTA/STS/16/31/2021–22) from the Department of Science and Technology, Govt of Karnataka, and SD thank CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru for seed money grant (SMSS 2101). The authors are grateful to Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bengaluru, for instrumentation facilities.

BSR thank Karnataka Science and Technology Academy (KSTA), for the Short-Term Studies grant (KSTA/STS/16/31/2021–22) from the Department of Science and Technology, Govt of Karnataka, and SD thank CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru for seed money grant (SMSS 2101). The authors are grateful to Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bengaluru, for instrumentation facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Powder XRD (b) FT-IR spectra of CF, CG, and CGD samples.
SEM images under different magnifications of (a,d) CF, (b,e) CG, (c,f) CGD, EDS spectra of (g) CF, (h) CG, (i) CGD and (j–n) elemental mapping of CGD.
(a–c) TEM images and (d) SAED pattern of CGD.
(a–e) XPS spectra of Co 2p, Fe 2p, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and (f) TGA curves of CF, CG, and CGD.
(a) LSV curves of CF, CG and CGD (b) overpotential at 10 mA cm−2 for CF, CG and CGD (c) Tafel plot derived from LSV (d) Nyquist plot obtained at a constant potential of −0.5 V for HER.

(a) TOF values (b) Mass activity obtained at various potential, CV for ESCA analysis of (c) CF (d) CGD at different scan rates (e) Linear plots of capacitive currents of the catalysts against scan rate (f) Bode plot at −0.45 V for HER.

(a) LSV curves of CF, CG, and CGD (b) Tafel plot derived from LSV (c) TOF values (d) Mass activity obtained at various potential (e) Nyquist plot obtained at a constant potential of 1.6 V (f) Bode plot at 1.6 V for OER.
(a) LSV curve of CGD for overall water splitting (b) Long-term durability of CGD for 2 h at 2.0 V.
Elemental composition of CF, CG, and CGD composite.
C dl and ECSA values for CF and CGD.

head.ce:title: N-doped graphene quantum dots incorporated cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride ternary composite for electrochemical overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to Be University), Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to Be University), Bengaluru, 560029, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicomponent electrocatalysts containing carbon supports play a crucial role in influencing the hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions which enhance the total water splitting. Herein, we report a ternary composite with cobalt ferrite, graphitic carbon nitride, and N-doped graphene quantum dots prepared via hydrothermal technique. The purity of the samples is established by carrying out various characterization methods. The intrinsic characteristics of the obtained materials are investigated by employing electrocatalytic processes in an alkaline media toward hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions. Cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N doped graphene quantum dots electrocatalyst demonstrates a very low overpotential towards hydrogen evolution reaction of 287 mV at a constant 10 mA cm−2 current density in 1.0 M KOH. Tafel slope and Rct values generated are 94 mV dec−1 and 0.86 cm2, respectively. Oxygen evolution reaction studies reveal an overpotential of 445 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with a Tafel slope of 69 mV dec−1. Finally, the cell potential needed for the cobalt ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N doped graphene quantum dots electrode to achieve 10 mA cm−2 in total water splitting is only 2.0 V while displaying long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are a dwindling resource, besides being responsible for the emission of greenhouse gases. The need for clean energy sources is therefore very critical, prompting academics to work on clean, sustainable, and alternative solutions for supplying energy. In this scenario, hydrogen fuel can be considered as a possible substitute for commercially accessible carbon releasing sources where its manufacture involves the reforming of fossil fuels, resulting in CO2 emissions [1]. A huge amount of work has gone into producing more environmentally friendly H2 sources by means of water electrolysis. However, because the method comprises of hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions (HER and OER), there is an increasing demand for developing electrocatalysts that limit any overpotential beyond the theoretical potential of 1.23 V [2–7]. Researchers have been focusing their efforts on evolving economical catalysts based on Mn, Fe, Co, and Ni transition metals [8–10], as well as their nitrides, oxides, chalcogenides, and phosphides [11,12], replacing the expensive benchmark electrocatalysts such as catalysts based on Pt–C, RuO2, and others for HER and OER [13–17].

Cobalt ferrite, which has a very low cost and outstanding catalytic activity, has emerged as a possible candidate for water splitting when electrocatalysts are properly considered [18–21]. Cobalt-based catalysts are especially desirable options because their efficiency may be significantly enhanced by the lattice inclusion of other metals [22,23]. Cobalt based spinels exhibit significant stability and charge transfer property, making them a suitable substrate for low-cost, easily scaled up electrocatalysts. CoFe2O4 with a partly inverted spinel structure has been primarily investigated as an OER catalyst [24], and its activity may be influenced by hybridization, shape, and size engineering. The electrocatalytic activity of spinel oxide catalysts can be increased further by adding anionic and cationic vacancies, a principle adopted successfully in graphene [25,26], and MoS2 [27].

The evolution of carbon-based metal-free water splitting catalysts has gained a lot of attraction recently due to their environmental friendliness, ease of availability, and low cost. Graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) is a common choice among them [4,7,28,29]. As a result, photocatalysts based on gCN have been extensively investigated for the photocatalytic destruction of a wide range of environmental contaminants, including antibiotics, hazardous organics, and other toxic dyes. Aside from photocatalytic CO2 reduction, gCN has also been employed as an electrocatalyst for H2 evolution [30–32].

According to a recent study, OER performance of transition metal compounds may be considerably enhanced by rationally modifying their morphology and electrical conductivity [33,34]. Constructing multicomponent catalysts is also a promising strategy for boosting OER electrocatalysis since the synergetic impact among diverse components can boost OER catalysts' electrocatalytic activity. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have captured the interest of many scientists owing to their fascinating characteristics, including strong biocompatibility, ease of preparation, exceptional optical properties, and environment friendliness [35]. Further, because of the abundance of functional groups like –OH, –COOH, –CO–, on their surface, GQDs may easily combine with transition metal oxides to form nanocomposites [36]. Based on the results obtained from other studies, GQDs play an important role in improving OER properties by influencing the shape and conductivity of composites [37]. The size of the nanoparticles, the method of synthesis, the nature, and quantity of dopants, all have a significant impact on the magnetic characteristics of cobalt ferrites. The optical, electrical, structural, and magnetic characteristics of the ferrites are modulated by these variables [33,38–40]. Moreover, due to the N-induced charge redistribution, nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) have higher catalytic activity in oxygen reduction reactions. On account of their great dispersion in water and ease of aggregation, a support is generally required to produce an effective and stable catalyst for both GQDs and NGQDs [41].

In the light of the above facts, we report here the modification of electrical conductivity and morphology of cobalt ferrite using gCN and NGQDs, as well as insight into the impact of NGQDs, that play a role in enhancing HER and OER performances. Such composites were mainly used for the degradation of different organic dyes and antibiotics since these are excellent photocatalysts. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that CoFe2O4/gCN/NGQDs with superior electrocatalytic performance have been reported. The CoFe2O4/gCN/NGQDs composite was prepared via the hydrothermal method and characterized for its structure and morphology using various techniques, while the electrochemical studies were done using a glassy carbon current collector. It is intriguing to note that the insertion of NGQDs effectively increases the electrical conductivity, significantly contributing to the electrocatalytic performance.

Materials essential for the experiments were ferric nitrate (98%), sodium hydroxide (98%), melamine (98%), citric acid (99%), ethylenediamine (98%), and ethanol (98%) of analytical grade and were purchased from SD Fine Chemicals. Nafion solution and cobalt nitrate (98%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cobalt ferrite/gCN/N doped graphene quantum dots (CGD)
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of cobalt ferrite (CF), hydrothermal method was employed where cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2.6H2O) and ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)2·9H2O) in the molar ratio 1:2 were dissolved thoroughly in distilled water kept in a magnetic stirrer for about 30 min. The solutions were mixed by constant stirring, and to this solution, 10 M NaOH solution was added dropwise in order to keep the pH at 12 and to obtain precipitates, while the stirring was continued for 2 h. The resultant mixture was kept in a muffle furnace maintained at 180 °C for 24 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The final product from the autoclave was cooled naturally and washed with ethanol and double-distilled water to get a black powder (CF) [42]. For preparing gCN, melamine was placed in a crucible and heated for 4 h at 550 °C in a muffle furnace to get the yellow colored powder which was used for the further preparation steps after grinding it into uniform size [43]. NGQDs were prepared from citric acid and ethylene diamine (1:1 ratio) via hydrothermal technique for about 6 h at 180 °C [44].

Binary composite of CF and gCN was synthesized using 2:1 ratio of CF and gCN, where both the components were mixed thoroughly for 15 min taken in an agate mortar. The obtained mixture was calcined at 400 °C for 4 h to form cobalt ferrite/gCN (CG). The mixture obtained was used for the preparation of ternary composite. Further, 1 g of CG and 5 mL of NGQDs solution were dissolved in distilled water and stirred for about 2 h to synthesize the ternary composite CGD, and the resultant mixture was kept for 7 h at 180 °C in a muffle furnace in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The solution obtained was filtered and washed with ethanol and distilled water to obtain a black powder of CGD.

The phase and crystal structure of the catalyst were studied using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker AXS Kappa Apex) using CuKα having wavelength 1.5418 A0 radiation in the range of 5–80°. The functional group was determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Alpha-T ATR-FTIR Bruker) in transmittance mode at 400–4000 cm−1. Thermal Analyzer STA 6000 PerkinElmer was used for thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive Analysis (EDS) (Quanta LV/ESEM) were utilized to study the elemental composition and surface morphology. PHI VersaProbe III type instrument Thermo-scientific and (JEOL Japan, JEM-2100 Plus) were used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and High-Resolution Transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) analysis respectively.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared material was studied on a CH1608 (CH Instruments Inc. USA) using a three-electrode system in 1 M KOH electrolyte. Sampling was done using 2 mg of catalyst in a mixture of 100 μL distilled water, 100 μL ethanol, and 100 μL Nafion solution under ultrasonication for 30 min. Drop casting of the prepared 5 μL of catalyst ink was done on top of the Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and kept for drying at room temperature for 1 h. The modified GCE acts as the working electrode, Hg/HgCl2 as the reference electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode were used for carrying out the studies. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves were recorded for all the samples at 5 mV s−1. The potential obtained from the analysis was converted to RHE using Nernst equation:
E
R H E
=
E
H g / H g C l 2
+
(
0.0591 × p H
)
+ 0.241
(pH of 1 M KOH is 14). Tafel slope was calculated, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were determined at a constant potential. Turnover frequency (TOF) and mass activity of all the samples were determined, and double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the materials was obtained by studying the impedance at different scan rates in the non-Faradaic regions.

To evaluate the phase and structure of the synthesized samples, XRD analysis was carried out (Fig. 1 a). For CF, CG, and CGD, all Miller indices at (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) of diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 18.3, 29.9, 34.38, 43.12, 53.4, 56.9, and 62.5° validate the face-centered cubic spinel single-phase cobalt ferrite from the XRD profile [42]. The strong diffraction peaks of the three samples demonstrate their exceptional crystallinity. There were no impurity phases in the pattern, confirming that the prepared samples were pure. The outcomes are quite similar to those of the CoFe2O4 JCPDS card number 00-022-1086 [42]. The XRD pattern obtained for the gCN and NGQDs depicts the characteristic peaks of 13.2 and 27.3° for gCN and 24.95° for NGQDs and is given in Fig. S1. A small peak is observed at 27.3° for (100) in the XRD pattern of CG, which corresponds to the presence of gCN [45]. No noticeable diffraction peaks were observed for gCN and NGQDs in CGD XRD pattern, which can be attributed to the fewer quantities and low crystallinity. Similar results have been reported earlier [46,47].

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) of the prepared samples show characteristic bands at 552 and 405 cm−1, confirming cobalt ferrite formation in all three samples [48]. The band at 1632 cm−1 relates to CO stretching vibration, and multiple bands at 1483 cm−1 stand for the stretching vibration of aromatic CC, and bands around 675 to 1000 cm−1 are due to the bending vibrations of = C–H [49]. The peaks noticed near 3280 and 3381 cm−1 relate to the N–H stretching, and 1064 cm−1 peak is attributed to the presence of aromatic CN/C–N of gCN in the CG, and CGD samples [50]. The presence of all these peaks in the FTIR spectrum of CGD vouches for the ternary nature of the composite.

Fig. 2 a–f show the SEM images of CF, CG, and CGD samples. The images clearly show nanoparticles with a spherical form and a nano-size dispersion. From the SEM images, it can be observed that the size of the particles increases from CF to CG and then to CGD. The size of the CF particles was found to be in the range of 12.29–18.86 nm, that of CG was 20.77–25.48 nm, while the CGD ternary composite exhibit a size of 34.77 nm. This can be attributed to the formation of composites with gCN and NGQDs.

The EDS spectra (Fig. 2) of CF, CG, and CGD samples depict the presence of different elements present in the materials and the corresponding percentage is given in Table 1 . The spectrum of CF shows the presence Co, Fe, and O elements with no other impurities present, whereas the CG and CGD samples contain only the Co, Fe, O, N, and C elements which give evidence to the purity of the samples and also confirming the presence of gCN and NGQDs incorporated in the ternary composite. The presence of elements Co, Fe, O, C, and N in the structure of CGD as seen in Fig. 2j–n is further confirmed by elemental mapping.

TEM analysis was used to examine the microstructures of 2D bulk gCN, CF, and NGQDs. Fig. 3 a and c show the internal architecture of the synthesized CGD sample. The darker portion in the TEM image of CGD shows that the bulk material is made up of a large number of randomly oriented layers of gCN covering each other, and shows cobalt ferrite and microscopic NGQDs formations dispersed over the gCN surface. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3d) points to the amorphous nature of the produced composite along with polycrystalline nature with the ring patterns.

The oxidation state, activation energy, and composition of the chemical compounds were determined using the XPS technique. The oxidation status of the unpaired electrons affect the system characteristics. Fig. 4 illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectra of the prepared CGD sample. Two peaks for Co2+ in the octahedral and tetrahedral sites are shown in the XPS data for the Co 2p3/2 level at 780.3 and 782.7 eV, respectively. The peaks at 796.7 eV for the as-prepared sample in Fig. 4a correspond to Co 2p1/2; consequently, the valence state of cobalt is Co2+ and the slight shift in the values can be attributed to the incorporation of gCN and NGQDs into the system [51]. Two peaks at 786.9 and 803.5 eV correspond to the satellite peak as previously reported. Comparably, Fig. 4b displays the deconvoluted peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 [52]. Fe3+ ions may be present in the octahedral and tetrahedral positions as shown by the 2p3/2 peak locations at 710.6 and 713.02 eV. According to findings, the binding energies of Fe3+ ions in the octahedral and tetrahedral sites are respectively, between 710.7-711.2 eV and 713.2–713.6 eV, which is compatible with the findings of the current work [53]. The peaks found at 724.3 correspond to the Fe 2p1/2 (Fig. 4b), confirming the presence of both Fe3+ and Fe2+ states [54].

Metal-bound oxygen is what leads the O 1s peak to appear around 530.16 eV and the binding energy peaks for 532.42 eV represent the presence of metal bonded to hydroxyl groups (Fig. 4c) [55]. The oxygen absorbed on the spinel's surface are attributed to the peaks at 534.7 eV [56]. The binding energy of N 1s at 398.2 eV shows the presence of metal nitrides present in the sample [57] (Fig. 4d). The peaks at 288.6 eV and 284.8 from the C 1s spectrum confirm the O–CO and C–C bondings present in the prepared sample [58] (Fig. 4e).

TGA analysis of the CF, CG, and CGD samples is given in Fig. 4f. The analysis was carried out from 25 °C to 800 °C while heating at 30 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. CF is stable till 800 °C without any weight loss. From our previous studies, it is clear that gCN has two different regions where it shows weight loss [59]. The first is from 100 to 400 °C due to surface adsorbed H2O, while the second weight-loss region is 550–750 °C, ascribed to the decomposition of the gCN.

CGD sample has a similar decomposition pattern, beginning at 500 °C and complete elimination of gCN occurring at 590 °C, indicating the inclusion of gCN in the binary composite. Further, until 800 °C, a plateau was found with no disintegration. The CGD TGA curve displays three weight-loss areas. The elimination of water, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide groups result in a continuous weight loss up to 300 °C. The breakdown from 300 to 550 °C explains the elimination of phenyl groups from NGQDs. The decomposition of gCN is responsible for the third weight loss area. The decomposition of gCN is delayed to 550 °C in this case. At 700 °C, the pyridine and pyrrolidine groups in NGQD decompose, but no further deterioration occurs until 800 °C [60].

At room temperature, the electrocatalytic characteristics of the produced samples were evaluated on HER with a three-electrode half-cell arrangement using 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. Modified GCE with all electrocatalysts were given the notations CF, CG, and CGD. Fig. 5 a shows the LSV polarization curves produced for GCE, CF, CG, and CGD with potential range of 0.0 to −1.7 V vs. RHE at 5 mV s−1. The HER activity on the bare GCE was minimal. As a result, during electrocatalytic processes, the activity of GCE had no effect towards activity of the modified electrocatalysts. During the HER experiments, CGD had an overpotential value of 287 mV at 10 mA cm−2(η 10) which is lesser than CG (319 mV(η 10) and CF (506 mV(η 10). The electrocatalytic activity of prepared samples was displayed in terms of overpotential (mV) at 10 mA/cm2 vs individual samples for better understanding (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c, representing the Tafel slope, indicates the rate-determining step and probable mechanism involved in electrocatalytic hydrogen generation. CF, CG, and CGD had Tafel slope values of 212, 153, and 96 mV dec−1 respectively. In order to study the mechanism from the Tafel slope data, the basic processes involved in the production of H2 must be mentioned.

It is necessary to discuss the various fundamental processes involved in H2 evolution. The mechanism of the process can be determined from the Tafel slope values. Production of hydrogen in alkaline media consists of 3 elementary steps given in Eq. (1) to Eq. (3):
(1)
∗ +
H 2
O +
e −
⇄ ∗
H
a d s
+ O
H −
(
V o l m e r
s t e p
)
(2)
∗
H
a d s
+
e −
+
H 2
O ⇄
H 2
+ O
H −
+ ∗
(
Heyrovsky
step
)
(3)
2 ∗
H
a d s
⇄
H 2
+ ∗
(
T a f e l
s t e p
)

Hads denotes the H2 adsorbed to the metal sites, where ∗ denotes the metal sites. The Volmer stage of the HER process starts with a discharge of proton (electrosorption process). Heyrovsky step is an electrochemical desorption step and is followed by the Tafel reaction. The entire response, however, comprises two processes: the Volmer–Heyrovsky and Volmer–Tafel mechanisms. Tafel slope values for the Tafel, Heyrovsky, and Volmer reaction steps are 30, 40, and 120 mV dec−1, respectively [61,62]. The catalytic activity of CGD targeting HER was quite high in the present investigation. It is possible that the poor performance of CF and CG toward HER is related to poor adsorption of H+ ions onto the material's surface [63]. Therefore, Heyrovsky reaction is the rate-determining step as concluded from the Tafel slope value for CGD and HER reaction follows a two-electron Volmer–Heyrovsky mechanism.

All samples were subjected to EIS studies in order to understand the charge-transfer kinetics taking place at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The obtained Nyquist plots from EIS measurements, at −0.5 V vs RHE, are shown in Fig. 5d. The first semicircle's high-end frequency area gives the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), which is an important criterion in determining the HER performance between the electrolyte and electrode. Rct values for CF, CG, and CGD were estimated to be 1.265, 1.857, and 0.8658 cm2, respectively. Rct was also investigated in order to understand the electrode kinetics better and to identify the best performing electrocatalyst at various potentials, which was found to be CGD composite. The charge transfer property of the CGD sample is accelerated by the applied potential, thereby reducing the solution resistance, as shown in Fig. 5d.

TOF was measured at various potential regions (−0.3 to 0.4 V vs. RHE) during HER to better understand the intrinsic activities of the materials. At 0.4 V vs. RHE, the obtained TOF for CGD was 9.53 s−1, which is much greater than that for CG (4.74 s−1) and CF (4.45 s−1) (Fig. 6 a). Fig. 6b shows the mass activity of each substance. Based on their performance during HER, the current (A) obtained per gram of electrocatalyst was investigated. Even at a potential range of −0.3 to −0.4 V, the improved performance of CGD vs. RHE was clearly visible; on the other side, CG had lesser activity than CGD while CF had a poorer performance against HER.

The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) is proportional to the electrocatalytic activity, and the cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was used to calculate the double-layer capacitance (Cdl in mF cm−2) where Cdl and ESCA are directly proportional to each other. In the case of CF and CGD, the Cdl is measured at various sweep speeds in non-faradaic regions in the potential range of 50–200 mV vs RHE (Fig. 6c and d). Cdl can be determined when Δj/2 is plotted against scan rate (Fig. 6e), (Δj/2 = (ja-jc)/2, where ja is the anodic current and jc is the cathodic current) (Table 1). The comparable slopes (Cdl) for CF and CGD are 0.0430 and 0.128 mF cm−2, respectively (Fig. 6e). The following formula may be used to compute ECSA: ECSA = Cdl/Cs, where Cs is the specific capacitance of the GCE (CS = 2 mF cm−2) [64]. With a ratio of 5.06 (0.109/0.0215), the comparable ECSA in the CGD composite appeared to be higher than CF (Table 2 ). This can be attributed to the dispersion of ferrite particles onto the gCN network, as the higher active surface area of the electrocatalyst causes more active sites for HER to become available.

As illustrated in Fig. 6f, electron lifetimes (τe) are calculated from the maximum frequency peak (fmax) of Bode plots analysed at a constant overpotential. The following equation was used to compute the lifespan of electrons: τe = 1/(2πfmax); where CF, CG, and CGD had fmax and estimated τe values of 1.99, 1.48, 2.78 Hz and 0.080, 0.107, and 0.0572 s, respectively. As a result, compared to other electrocatalytic materials, CGD had a lower charge-transfer resistance and shorter relaxation time, resulting in increased HER activity [65]. Since nitrogen doped graphene has superior electrical conductivity, porous nature, and a high surface area, the nitrogen-containing gCN and NGQDs will be crucial in enhancing the CGD sample's electrochemical properties.

The OER activity of CF, CG, and CGD samples was determined at room temperature using a 1.0 M KOH solution. The GCE modified with CF, CG, and CGD was activated at a saturation current point using the CV. LSV technique was employed to analyse the prepared samples towards the OER activities at 5 mV s−1 scan rate. The measurements for bare GCE too were performed in order to compare with the samples. Fig. 7 a shows the LSV curves of CF, CG, and CGD obtained during OER run. Further, the performance of the electrocatalyst was analysed precisely to identify the catalyst, which gives the standard current density of 10 mA cm−2. Using the equation: η = ERHE - 1.23, overpotential (η) was determined at 10 mA cm−2 current density (CD), where ERHE is the experimental potential at 10 mA cm−2. In addition, the overpotential of CGD at 10 mA cm−2 was 445 mV (η10), which was lower than the overpotentials of CG and CF. As a result, CGD demonstrated superior catalytic efficacy toward OER while having a smaller overpotential.

The polarization curves were utilized to obtain the Tafel slopes of the samples in order to gain more details regarding the electrocatalytic behavior. Fig. 7b shows the Tafel slopes of CF, CG, and CGD samples, and the values attained are 331, 191, and 69 mV dec−1, respectively. The lowest value for the CGD sample portrays the enhanced electrocatalytic behavior and kinetics towards OER. The general process that occurs in an alkaline medium during OER is outlined in Eqs. (4)–(7), based on prior investigations.
(4)
∗ + O
H −
→
e −
+ O
H ∗
(5)
O
H −
+ O
H ∗
→
e −
+
H 2
O
(6)
O
H −
+
O ∗
→
e −
+ O O
H ∗
(7)
O
H −
+ O O
H ∗
→
H 2
O + ∗ +
e −
+
O 2
where ∗ denotes the electrocatalyst's adsorption site, similarly, during OER, the adsorbed intermediates are OH∗, O∗, and OOH∗. TOF displays a direct comparison of the materials' inherent catalytic activity towards OER (Fig. 7c). In comparison to CG (20.099 s−1) and CF (18.927 s−1), the estimated TOF values show that CGD has the maximum TOF value at 1.7 V vs. RHE (81.097 s−1). The highly accessible active sites in CGD's sample were responsible for its activity. Fig. 7d shows the electrocatalyst activity per gram of material.

The Nyquist plot obtained, as shown in Fig. 7e, comprises of the solution resistance (Rs) and the resistance between electrolyte and electrode (Rct). In 1.0 M KOH, EIS measurements were done at 1.6 V vs. RHE constant potential. As charge transfer rate rises, the radius of the semicircle decreases in the EIS spectra. CGD, CG, and CF have Rs values of 12.18, 33.43, and 16.05 Ω cm−2 respectively. The improved performance of CGD in HER and OER is regulated by two primary aspects in this study: electrochemically accessible active sites and material intrinsic properties. According to calculations, CGD has a mass activity of 28.5 A g−1 at 1.7 V vs. RHE, which is greater than CG and CF. Further, as shown in Fig. 7f, the Bode curve obtained at a comparable overpotential value at 1.6 V vs. RHE obviously shows that CGD has a shorter relaxation time (0.0415 s) than CG (0.045 s) and CF (0.46 s). EIS studies were also carried out further to know the prepared materials' charge-transfer capabilities.

Systems for water splitting could be made simpler and more affordable by using a bifunctional electrocatalyst that has good activity for both HER and OER efficiency and typically affects the overall kinetics of water splitting. The distinctive morphologies with well-defined connections to the substrate, which offer many reaction sites and encourage electron transfer, are responsible for the good catalytic capabilities. An overall water electrolysis system is built using CGD as a bifunctional catalyst for both the anode and cathode sides because of its high activity and stability in 1.0 M alkaline solutions for both HER and OER. Hydrogen and oxygen bubbles are produced in the cathode and anode electrodes during the electrolysis, and the CGD electrocatalyst only requires a 2.0 V cell potential to attain a CD of 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. 8 a). According to the results, the CGD electrocatalyst can be the best bifunctional material for HER and OER applications. In addition, the CGD electrode was subjected to a 2 h stability assessment at a constant voltage of 2.0 V. CGD has an initial current of 10.2 mA cm−2 as seen in Fig. 8b. As the reaction progresses, the modified electrodes exhibit a steady development of H2 and O2. CGD can still create a CD of 9.68 mA cm−2 after 2 h of reaction, demonstrating CGD's long-term stability. The durability and large surface area produced by the graphitic carbon nitride, NGQDs, and metal center make this ternary composite (CGD) highly efficient for the electrocatalytic generation of hydrogen energy by water electrolysis.

A ternary composite of CoFe2O4/gCN/NGQDs (CGD) was synthesized hydrothermally and was confirmed by various physicochemical methods. The formation of the ternary composite, the valence state of each element, and the presence of gCN and NGQDs are clearly observed from XRD and XPS studies. In an alkaline medium, the electrochemical reaction of these materials to HER and OER was investigated. The CGD composite displayed remarkable electrocatalytic performance towards both HER and OER with an overpotential of 287 and 445 mV at 10 mA cm−2 CD. The overall water splitting cell obtained a CD of 10 mA cm−2 at a low cell voltage of 2.0 V with long-run stability. Therefore, improved activity of CGD towards water electrolysis can be related to the durability and high surface area achieved from the graphitic carbon nitride, NGQDs, and metal centers which make this ternary composite very effective for the generation of sustainable and clean hydrogen energy through electrocatalysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: BSR thank Karnataka Science and Technology Academy (KSTA), for the Short-Term Studies grant (KSTA/STS/16/31/2021–22) from the Department of Science and Technology, Govt of Karnataka, and SD thank CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru for seed money grant (SMSS 2101). The authors are grateful to Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bengaluru, for instrumentation facilities.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2022.10.169.
